skill.name: ios-debugger-agent
skill.description: Uses XcodeBuildMCP to build, run, inspect, and debug iOS apps on a booted simulator.
skill.summary: Debug iOS simulator builds and runtime behavior with XcodeBuildMCP.
skill.author: Dimillian

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, upload, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but the file is documentation for XcodeBuildMCP tool calls rather than executable shell or Ruby code. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, weak cryptography, or upload target was found. The skill is medium risk because it can build, launch, interact with, screenshot, and collect logs from an iOS simulator.

medium_findings.title: Simulator Control and Diagnostic Capture
medium_findings.description: The skill directs use of XcodeBuildMCP to discover a booted simulator, build and launch apps, interact with UI controls, take screenshots, and capture logs. This is legitimate debugger behavior, but it can expose app state or user-entered test data if used outside an explicit debugging request.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly instructs simulator control, UI interaction, screenshot capture, and log capture through named MCP tools. The use is aligned with the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Backtick Command Detections Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick execution findings are false positives. The backticks wrap MCP tool names, parameter names, and configuration values in Markdown, and no executable script or shell interpolation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are Markdown bullets that name MCP tools and arguments. No code file, shell command string, Ruby execution, or user-controlled command construction exists in the scanned skill.

low_findings.title: Upload and Weak Crypto Blockers Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-risk screen capture upload and weak cryptographic algorithm findings are not supported by the file contents. The skill mentions logs, UI description, and debugging, but it does not define an upload destination, network call, hash algorithm, cipher, or cryptographic operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain natural-language debugging instructions and an MCP UI description tool name. I found no evidence of upload behavior or any cryptographic primitive.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Scoped to the Simulator
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance finding is a low-risk false positive. The cited workflow resolves an app path and bundle identifier only when the simulator app bundle id is unknown, which is normal iOS debugging context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are limited to simulator app metadata needed for launching and logging. No host enumeration, credential discovery, persistence, or broad system inventory behavior appears in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: External MCP Tool Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill relies on external XcodeBuildMCP operations that can build projects, launch apps, perform UI actions, take screenshots, and collect logs. These operations should run only with user consent and against the intended simulator and app scheme.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The tool names and workflow are explicit in the skill file. They are not malicious by themselves, but they cross from advice into simulator automation and diagnostic data collection.

content.value_statement: iOS simulator debugging often requires repeated build, launch, UI inspection, and log steps. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused workflow for using XcodeBuildMCP safely.
actual_capabilities: Finds the currently booted iOS simulator before running a workflow.
Sets XcodeBuildMCP session defaults for project, scheme, and simulator.
Builds and runs the current iOS app on the selected simulator.
Launches an already built app when only startup is needed.
Inspects and interacts with simulator UI elements through MCP tools.
Captures simulator logs and console output for debugging summaries.
limitations: Requires XcodeBuildMCP tools to be available in the assistant environment.
Requires at least one simulator to be booted by the user.
Does not choose a project scheme unless the repository makes it clear.
Does not replace human review of sensitive logs or screenshots.

use_cases.title: Run a Local iOS App
use_cases.description: Build and launch the current project on a booted simulator, then report build or launch problems.

use_cases.title: Inspect Runtime UI State
use_cases.description: Describe the visible simulator UI before taps, swipes, or text entry during a debugging session.

use_cases.title: Summarize Simulator Logs
use_cases.description: Start and stop simulator log capture, then summarize important errors and console output.

prompt_templates.title: Run the App
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iOS debugger agent to find the booted simulator, build the current app, run it, and summarize any errors.

prompt_templates.title: Launch and Inspect
prompt_templates.prompt: Launch the already built iOS app on the booted simulator, describe the current UI, and tell me what screen is visible.

prompt_templates.title: Reproduce a UI Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the iOS debugger agent to open the app, inspect the UI before each action, tap through the reported flow, and capture relevant logs.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Runtime Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Set the XcodeBuildMCP session defaults, run the app with console capture, reproduce the issue, then summarize the strongest log evidence.

output_examples.input: Run the current iOS app on the simulator.
output_examples.output: Found one booted simulator, selected the current app scheme, and started the build and run workflow. The app launched successfully, and no critical log errors appeared.

output_examples.input: Check why the login screen is stuck.
output_examples.output: The visible UI shows the login screen with the submit button disabled. The recent logs point to missing validation state after text entry.

output_examples.input: Capture console output while I reproduce the crash.
output_examples.output: Log capture is active for the app bundle. After the crash, the summary highlights the first exception message and the last relevant console events.

best_practices: Boot the intended simulator before invoking the workflow.
Confirm the project scheme and bundle identifier when more than one app exists.
Review screenshots and logs for sensitive data before sharing them.
anti_patterns: Do not run UI actions against an unclear simulator or app scheme.
Do not capture logs for unrelated apps or unrelated user sessions.
Do not use coordinate taps until UI labels or element identifiers fail.

faq.question: Does this skill install Xcode or simulators?
faq.answer: No. It assumes Xcode, the project, and XcodeBuildMCP are already available.

faq.question: Can it boot a simulator automatically?
faq.answer: The workflow asks the user to boot a simulator unless the user explicitly requests booting.

faq.question: Does it upload screenshots or logs?
faq.answer: No upload behavior is defined in the skill. It can request simulator screenshots and logs through MCP tools.

faq.question: Can it choose the right scheme?
faq.answer: It uses the current app scheme when clear. Ambiguous repositories may require user confirmation.

faq.question: Is this useful for SwiftUI apps?
faq.answer: Yes. It can build, run, inspect UI state, and collect logs for simulator-based SwiftUI debugging.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill controls a simulator and can collect screenshots and logs, so it should be used only for intended debugging sessions.
